topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the brutal finality of a gavel strike. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah has been cancelled." My fingers hovered over the keyboard, the screen reflecting a stark, unwelcome brightness. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like spilled ink. I reread the message three times, searching for a typo, a mistake, anything to rationalize the sudden vacuum where excitement should have been. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. Every surface seemed too bright, too loud. I walked to the window, peering out at the mundane street, feeling a profound sense of deflation wash over me, leaving behind a hollow ache.

[story 2]

When I saw the notification pop up, my breath hitched. It wasn't a subtle cancellation; it was a blunt, official dismissal. A wave of nausea rolled over me, making my vision swim slightly. I stared at the screen, my hands trembling so badly I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady myself. The carefully constructed anticipation that had been building for weeks—the planning, the secret coordination, the giddy hope—evaporated instantly, leaving behind a raw, exposed vulnerability. I let out a small, involuntary sound, a sharp intake of air, and then I just sat down heavily on the sofa, the fabric rough against my skin, feeling utterly unmoored and heavy.

[story 3]

I picked up my phone, intending to call the organizer, but my thumb froze above the dial button. The words on the screen—*cancelled*—felt like a physical blow. A strange, buzzing emptiness settled in my chest, a sensation akin to having someone abruptly pull the plug on a vital machine. I slowly lowered the phone, the silence rushing back in, thick and suffocating. I started rubbing my temples, the pressure behind my eyes mounting until it felt like a headache was about to erupt. I walked into the kitchen, the fluorescent light seeming harsh and unforgiving, and simply leaned against the counter, my gaze fixed on a spot on the tile floor, unable to muster the energy to move or speak.
